instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
Budget and Changes in Direct Taxes
The 2026 Union Budget presented by Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman focused on simplifying India’s tax laws, easing compliance, and modernising the direct tax regime. Rather than large rate cuts for individuals, the Budget introduced targeted changes to tax structures, exemptions, TDS/TCS rules, the new Income Tax Act, STT, and corporate tax norms. These are meant to reduce disputes, encourage compliance, and support investment and growth in key areas. Read ahead to know the suggested changes in detail.
Key Changes in Direct Tax Rules from Budget 2026
Here are some major changes suggested by the FM related to direct taxes in Budget 2026:
New Income Tax Act
The existing Income Tax law is replaced by a simplified Income Tax Act, 2025, effective from April 1, 2026. This act is designed to be clearer, easier to understand, and to reduce litigation.
The revised return deadline has been extended from 31 December to 31 March, with a nominal fee.
ITR-1 and ITR-2 filers can continue filing till 31 July.
Non-audit businesses and trusts may file up to 31 August.
Taxpayers may update returns even after reassessment begins by paying 10% extra tax over the normal rate.
One-time 6-month foreign asset disclosure window for small taxpayers below a specified limit.
Relief and Exemptions
Interest received from Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (MACT) awards will be fully exempt from income tax for natural persons, and TDS on such interest will be removed.
Immunity from prosecution (retrospective from 1 Oct 2024) for non-disclosure of small foreign assets (value < ₹20 lakh).
Penalty-free and prosecution-free framework now extended from under-reporting to certain misreporting cases.
Certain offences like non-production of books or delay in TDS payment are now decriminalised.
Corporate Tax and MAT Changes
The Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) rate is reduced and restructured to make it a final tax for companies shifting to the new regime.
MAT credit set-off allowed up to 1/4th of the tax liability.
MAT credits from earlier years can be partly carried forward (particularly for domestic companies).
Non-residents under presumptive taxation are fully exempt from MAT.
A Joint Committee (MCA + CBDT) will integrate ICDS (Income Computation and Disclosure Standards) into Indian Accounting Standards, reducing mismatches between accounting and tax books.
TDS and TCS Rationalisation
Tax Collected at Source (TCS) on overseas tour packages cut to 2%, with no minimum amount condition.
TCS on Liberalised Remittance Scheme (LRS) for education and medical purposes reduced from 5% to 2%.
Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) on manpower supply services will be 1% or 2%.
TDS on property purchased from a non-resident will now be deposited using the resident buyer’s PAN, removing the TAN requirement.
Depositories can now accept Form 15G/15H for taxpayers who hold securities across multiple companies.
Small taxpayers can get lower/nil TDS certificates automatically through a rule-based system.
Capital Gains & Share Buyback Changes
Share buyback proceeds will now be taxed under capital gains instead of dividend income.
This brings consistency and clarity to how gains from buybacks are taxed.
Securities Transaction Tax (STT) Increase
STT on futures contracts will increase from 0.02% to 0.05%.
For options, the STT will go up as well. The premium will now attract 0.15% instead of 0.1%, and the rate on exercising an option will also increase to 0.15% from 0.125%.
No Change in Personal Income Tax Slabs
Personal income tax slabs remained unchanged in 2026.
This gives taxpayers continuity and a sense of predictability.
Conclusion
Budget 2026 is built around the principles of clarity and simplification. The government has lowered TDS/TCS burdens, eased return filing rules, expanded exemptions, and modernised MAT and buyback taxation. The one-time foreign asset disclosure, decriminalisation of minor offences, and smoother processes indicate a strong shift toward trust-based tax administration. Overall, the Budget aims to reduce friction, cut complexity, and give taxpayers more control and certainty.
Reference:
https://static.pib.gov.in/WriteReadData/specificdocs/documents/2026/feb/doc202621775901.pdf
Disclaimer
The content provided is for general informational purposes only. The material is compiled from publicly available sources, internal insights, and other information deemed reliable. While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. The opinions expressed do not constitute formal recommendations, professional advice, or definitive guidance. Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before making any financial or investment decisions based on the content. Any illustrations included are for conceptual clarity only and do not reflect the actual performance of any product or offering.
Tax benefits as per prevailing Income tax laws shall apply. Please check with your tax consultant for eligibility

output.final_text:
Budget and Changes in Direct Taxes
The 2026 Union Budget presented focused on simplifying India’s tax laws, easing compliance, and modernising the direct tax regime. The Budget introduced targeted changes to tax structures, exemptions, TDS/TCS rules, the new Income Tax Act, STT, and corporate tax norms. These are meant to reduce disputes, encourage compliance, and support investment and growth in key areas. Read ahead to know the suggested changes in detail.
Key Changes in Direct Tax Rules from Budget 2026*
Here are some major changes related to direct taxes in Budget 20261:
● New Income Tax Act 2005 o The existing Income Tax law is replaced by a simplified Income Tax Act, 2025,
effective from April 1, 2026. This act is designed to be clearer, easier to understand, and to reduce litigation. o The revised return deadline has been extended from 31 December to 31 March,
with a nominal fee. o ITR-1 and ITR-2 filers can continue filing till 31 July. o Non-audit businesses and trusts may file up to 31 August. o Taxpayers may update returns even after reassessment begins by paying 10% extra
tax over the normal rate. o One-time 6-month window to disclose foreign assets under the o Foreign Assets of Small Taxpayers - Disclosure Scheme, 2026 (FAST-DS 2026)
● Relief and Exemptions o Interest received from Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (MACT) awards as
compensation amount will be fully exempt from income tax for natural persons, and TDS on such interest will be removed. o Immunity from prosecution (retrospective from 1 Oct 2024) under The Black Money
(Undisclosed Foreign Income and Assets) and Imposition of Tax Act, 2015, for non- disclosure of small foreign assets (value < ₹20 lakh) under the Foreign Assets of Small Taxpayers - Disclosure Scheme, 2026 (FAST-DS 2026). o Penalty-free and prosecution-free framework now extended from under-reporting
to certain misreporting cases. However, in such case, taxpayers would have to pay 100% of the tax liability as an additional income tax over and above the outstanding amount of income tax and interest on it. o Certain offences like non-production of books and documents or and requirement of
TDS payment where payment is mad in kind are now decriminalised.
● Corporate Tax and MAT Changes o The Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) rate is reduced and restructured to make it a
final tax for companies shifting to the new regime. o MAT credit set-off allowed up to 1/4th of the tax liability in the new regime. o MAT credits from earlier years can be partly carried forward (up to 1/4th) only in the
new regime o Non-residents under presumptive taxation are fully exempt from MAT. o A Joint Committee (Ministry of Corporate Affairs + Central Board of Direct Taxes) will
integrate ICDS (Income Computation and Disclosure Standards) into Indian Accounting Standards, reducing mismatches between accounting and tax books.
● TDS and TCS Rationalisation o Tax Collected at Source (TCS) on overseas tour packages cut to 2%. o TCS on Liberalised Remittance Scheme (LRS) for education and medical purposes
reduced from 5% to 2%. o Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) on manpower supply services will be 1% or 2%. o TDS on property purchased from a non-resident will now be deposited using the
resident buyer’s PAN-based challan, removing the TAN requirement. o Depositories can now accept Form 15G/15H for taxpayers who hold securities across
multiple companies. o Small taxpayers can get lower/nil TDS certificates automatically through a rule-based
automated process instead of filing application with the Assessing Officer.
● Capital Gains & Share Buyback Changes o Share buyback proceeds will now be taxed under capital gains instead of dividend
income. o This brings consistency and clarity to how gains from buybacks are taxed.
● Securities Transaction Tax (STT) Increase o STT on futures contracts will increase from 0.02% to 0.05%. o For options, the STT will go up as well. The premium will now attract 0.15% instead
of 0.1%, and the rate on exercising an option will also increase to 0.15% from 0.125%.
● No Change in Personal Income Tax Slabs o Personal income tax slabs remained unchanged in 2026. o This gives taxpayers continuity and a sense of predictability.
Conclusion
Budget 2026 is built around the principles of clarity and simplification. The government has lowered TDS/TCS burdens, eased return filing rules, expanded exemptions, and modernised MAT and buyback taxation. The one-time foreign asset disclosure, decriminalisation of minor offences, and smoother processes indicate a strong shift toward trust-based tax administration. Overall, the Budget aims to reduce friction, cut complexity, and give taxpayers more control and certainty.

01.pdf
